Chapter 25
Amino Acids, Peptides, and Proteins

Amino Acids and Polypeptides
Amino acids contain an amino group and a carboxylic acid group
The amino and acid groups can bond to form an amide; the resulting linkage is called a peptide bond
Polypeptides or proteins are polymers of amino acids
The structural and functional diversity of proteins is huge !

Section 25.1
CLASSIFICATION OF AMINO ACIDS

Distance Between Amine and Acid
A Greek letter is used to designate the position of the amino group on the carbon chain bearing the acid
Naturally occuring amino acids are (almost) universally α -amino acids

Characteristics of Amino Acids
There are 20 amino acids that occur naturally in proteins
12 can be synthesized by the body
8 must be obtained from the diet
The standard amino acids differ in characteristics:
Size and shape
Polarity
Acidity/basicity
The side chains are the key points of difference between the amino acids
Amino Acids with Nonpolar Side Chains
Polar but Non-ionized Side Chains
Acidic Side Chains
Basic Side Chains

Nonstandard Amino Acids
Biochemical modifications can produce nonstandard amino acids
Some have also been artificially incorporated into synthetic proteins
Remarkably, DNA codes for these two nonstandard amino acids :

Section 25.2
STEREOCHEMISTRY OF AMINO ACIDS

Configurations of Amino Acids
Glycine ( R=H ) is the only achiral amino acid
The α -carbon is a stereocenter in most amino acids
Amino acids in proteins have the l configuration, but d - amino acids do occur naturally
Amino group at the left when the carboxyl group is at the top of the Fischer projection
Isoleucine and threonine include a second stereocenter in the side chain .

Section 25.3
ACID-BASE BEHAVIOR OF AMINO ACIDS

Acid-base Behavior of Glycine
The neutral formula of glycine suggests that it should be soluble in nonpolar solvents …but it isn’t!
Glycine includes an acidic functional group (carboxylic acid) and a basic functional group (amine)
The form favored in neutral solution ( pH 7 ) is a zwitterion
How does the dominant ionization state of glycine change with pH? Hint: it is never neutral !

Titration of Glycine
Respect pK a . Draw amino acids in solution as zwitterions, not neutral molecules .
− CO 2 − is deprotonated “first” …
…then − NH 3 + is deprotonated !

p K a Values and Isoelectric Point
Amino acid p K a values are influenced by the structure of the side chain and differ slightly from one another
The pH at which [zwitterion] is maximal is called the isoelectric point (pI)
I.e., the pH at which the amino acid has no net charge
The isoelectric point is the equivalence point of the titration!

p K a Values and Isoelectric Point-1
TABLE 25.2 Acid-Base Properties of Amino Acids with Neutral Side Chains
Amino acid | PKa1* | PKa2* | pl
Glycine | 2.34 | 9.60 | 5.97
Alanine | 2.34 | 9.69 | 6.00
Valine | 2.32 | 9.62 | 5.96
Leucine | 2.36 | 9.60 | 5.98
Isoleucine | 2.36 | 9.60 | 6.02
Methionine | 2.28 | 9.21 | 5.74
Proline | 1.99 | 10.60 | 6.30
Phenylalanine | 1.83 | 9.13 | 5.48
Tryptophan | 2.83 | 9.39 | 5.89
Asparagine | 2.02 | 8.80 | 5.41
Glutamine | 2.17 | 9.13 | 5.65
Serine | 2.21 | 9.15 | 5.68
Threonine | 2.09 | 9.10 | 5.60
Tyrosine | 2.20 | 9.11 | 5.66
*In all cases, p K a1 corresponds to ionization of the carboxyl group; p K a2 corresponds to deprotonation of the ammonium ion .

Acidic and Basic Side Chains
Some amino acids have acidic side chains–they are deprotonated at pH 7!
Others have basic side chains — they are protonated at pH 7!
pK a values differ somewhat from typical functional group values

Acidic and Basic Side Chains-1
TABLE 25.3 Acid-Base Properties of Amino Acids with lonizable Side Chains
Amino acid | pKa1* | pKa2* | pKa of side chain | pl
Aspartic acid | 1.88 | 9.60 | 3.65 | 2.77
Glutamic acid | 2.19 | 9.67 | 4.25 | 3.22
Lysine | 2.18 | 8.95 | 10.53 | 9.74
Arginine | 2.17 | 9.4** | 13.8** | 11.6**
Histidine | 1.82 | 9.17 | 6.00 | 7.59
*In all cases, p K a1 corresponds to ionization of the carboxyl group of RCHCO 2 H and p K a2 to ionization of the ammonium ion.

Section 25.4
SYNTHESIS OF AMINO ACIDS

Nucleophilic Substitution
Ammonia reacts with α - haloacids to give α - amino acids in a nucleophilic substitution reaction
Note the need for two equivalents of ammonia, to deprotonate the acid and engage in substitution

Strecker Synthesis
The cyanide anion (CN − ) is a “masked” version of a carboxylate (CO 2 − )
Addition of cyanide to an imine followed by hydrolysis gives an α -amino acid!
The imine is generated in situ from an aldehyde
Amenable to enantioselective catalysis

α- Amino Malonates
Direct alkylation of amino acid enolates with RX is impractical. But we’ve seen this before for other enolates!
Application of the malonic ester synthesis to α -amino malonates works well and follows the spirit of enolate alkylation
Deprotonation of carbonyl compound and alkylation with RX. The amine should be protected as N -acetyl

𝛂-Amino Malonates-1
Direct alkylation of amino acid enolates with RX is impractical. But we’ve seen this before for other enolates!
Application of the malonic ester synthesis to α -amino malonates works well and follows the spirit of enolate alkylation
Hydrolysis to free the amine and carboxylates
Heat to promote decarboxylation

Section 25.5
REACTIONS OF AMINO ACIDS

Reactions of Amino Acids
Amino acids undergo many of the reactions of amines and carboxylic acids:
Acetylation of N. Very important for suppressing the nucleophilicity of nitrogen (protecting group !)
Different deprotection methods are available for amides of these groups :

Section 25.6
PEPTIDES

Peptides and Peptide Bonds
The amino group is a nucleophile and the carboxyl group (appropriately activated) an electrophile
Coupling to form an amide is known as peptide coupling ; the amide is called a peptide bond
The free amine is the N - terminus (left) and the free carboxylate is the C -terminus (right)
Each amino acid is called a residue ; the sequence of residues is called the primary sequence

Geometry of the Peptide Bond
Recall that amides are planar — the nitrogen is sp 2 hybridized !
Rotation about the C (=O )–N bond is fairly difficult. Can you draw resonance structures to illustrate why?
Hydrogen bonding of N–H and C=O (with backbone and side chains ) is extremely important for protein structure

Disulfide Bonds & Covalent Linkages
The disulfide bond is an important covalent linkage in proteins
Two cysteine residues are oxidized from 2 SH to S–S
An important structural feature in the hormone oxytocin
Side-chain amides (e.g. lysine + glutamic acid) are also observed in some protein structures (much more rare, however )

Section 25.7
INTRODUCTION TO PEPTIDE STRUCTURE DETERMINATION

Determining Polypeptide Sequence
Determining primary sequence is tough
Polypeptides contain many copies of the same type of bond
Selective cleavage is difficult
Structural differences between amino acids are subtle
Several different methods are involved in rigorous determination of peptide sequence
Determine what amino acids are present and their molar ratios.
Cleave the peptide into smaller fragments, separate these fragments, and determine the amino acid composition of the fragments.
Identify the N-terminal and the C-terminal amino acid in the original peptide and in each fragment.
Organize the information so that the amino acid sequences of small fragments can be overlapped to reveal the full sequence .
